title: Model Versioning: Tracking the Champions
concept: Model versioning tracks each trained model as a distinct, labeled version, allowing teams to compare performance and instantly roll back if a new model performs worse.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What commonly causes a model to behave subtly differently when moved between machines?

options.text: A mismatch in the software library versions used to save vs. load the model.
The model file becoming physically heavier.
Using a different color scheme on the server dashboard.

exercises.explain: Correct! Library version mismatches are the classic culprit behind portability bugs.

cards.title: Save slots in a video game
cards.body: A careful gamer never overwrites their only save file before a risky boss fight — they create a new save slot first. If the fight goes badly, they can instantly reload the older, safer save. Machine learning teams use the exact same strategy with model versions.

cards.title: Model Versioning
cards.body: Model versioning is the practice of saving each trained model as a distinct, labeled file (e.g. fraud_model_v1.joblib, fraud_model_v2.joblib) rather than overwriting the previous one, allowing teams to track and compare every iteration.

cards.title: Draft history in a document editor
cards.body: Versioning a model is like a document editor's 'version history' feature. If your latest edit accidentally deletes an important paragraph, you don't panic — you simply restore an earlier saved draft. Model versions give teams that same safety net.

cards.title: The champion-challenger pattern
cards.body: A team keeps v3 (the current 'champion' model) running live in production, while testing v4 (the 'challenger') on a small slice of traffic. If v4 performs better on real metrics, it's promoted to become the new champion. If not, v3 keeps running unaffected.

cards.title: A newer model isn't automatically a better one
cards.body: You might assume the most recently trained model is always the best choice to deploy. In reality, a new model can sometimes perform worse due to a data quality issue or an unexpected bug. Versioning ensures you always have a proven, working fallback to roll back to instantly.

cards.title: Model Versioning in Action
cards.body: How teams manage the risk of deploying updates:

left.text:
Currently live in production.
Proven, stable performance.

right.text:
Newly trained candidate.
Tested before full promotion.

exercises.prompt: What is the main purpose of model versioning?

options.text: To track each trained model as a distinct file, enabling comparison and instant rollback if needed.
To automatically delete all older, less accurate models.
To merge every trained model into a single averaged file.
To compress model files to reduce disk usage.

exercises.explain: Correct! Versioning tracks distinct models so teams can compare and roll back safely.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The most recently trained model version is always guaranteed to perform better than the previous version.
exercises.explain: False! A newer model can sometimes underperform due to bugs or data issues, which is exactly why versioning and rollback matter.

exercises.sentence: In the champion-challenger pattern, the currently live production model is called the ___.
exercises.explain: The 'champion' is the current production model, while new candidates are 'challengers'.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario reflects good model versioning practice?

options.text: A team saves each new model as 'model_v5.joblib' instead of overwriting 'model_v4.joblib', keeping both available.
A team saves every new model with the exact same filename, permanently overwriting the previous version.
A team deletes all previous model files immediately after training a new one.

exercises.explain: Correct! Keeping distinct, labeled versions instead of overwriting is the core practice of model versioning.

exercises.prompt: A team deploys a newly trained fraud model (v6), replacing the previous v5. Within an hour, monitoring shows v6 is flagging far too many legitimate transactions as fraud. What should the team do, and why does versioning matter here?

options.text: Roll back to the previously saved v5 model file immediately, since v6 is clearly underperforming and v5 is a proven, working fallback.
Do nothing, since the newest model is always automatically the correct choice.
Delete both v5 and v6 and start training completely from scratch.
Wait several months before taking any action.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Because the team kept v5 as a distinct saved version rather than overwriting it, they can roll back to it instantly, avoiding prolonged damage from the underperforming v6. Congratulations on completing the Model Serialization unit! Next, let's learn how to actually serve these models to the outside world!
